section.title: The Transfiguration
section.sectionRationale: Six days after the confession and the first passion prediction, Jesus is unveiled in glory on the mountain, with the Father's own voice confirming Peter's confession. Then comes the descent into a world of unbelief, a failed exorcism, a second passion prediction, and a quiet lesson about the temple tax.
section.thesis: On the mountain the Father unveils the glory of His beloved Son and commands, Listen to Him, confirming that the suffering Christ is the radiant Lord; and back in the valley that same Son heals what faithless disciples cannot, foretells His death again, and shows Himself the Son who is free yet stoops to pay.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the transfiguration and the Father's voice; the coming of Elijah in John the Baptist; the boy with a demon and the power of faith; the second passion prediction; and the temple tax.

verses.text: After six days Jesus took with Him Peter, James, and John the brother of James, and led them up a high mountain by themselves.
There He was {{metamorphoo|transfigured}} before them. His face shone like the sun, and His clothes became as white as the light.
Suddenly Moses and Elijah appeared before them, talking with Jesus.
Peter said to Jesus, “Lord, it is good for us to be here. If You wish, I will put up three shelters, one for You, one for Moses, and one for Elijah.”
While Peter was still speaking, a bright cloud enveloped them, and a voice from the cloud said, “This is My {{agapetos|beloved}} Son, in whom I am well pleased. Listen to Him!”
When the disciples heard this, they fell facedown in terror.
Then Jesus came over and touched them. “Get up,” He said. “Do not be afraid.”
And when they looked up, they saw no one except Jesus.
As they were coming down the mountain, Jesus commanded them, “Do not tell anyone about this vision until the Son of Man has been raised from the dead.”
The disciples asked Him, “Why then do the scribes say that Elijah must come first?”
Jesus replied, “Elijah does indeed come, and he will restore all things.
But I tell you that Elijah has already come, and they did not recognize him, but have done to him whatever they wished. In the same way, the Son of Man will suffer at their hands.”
Then the disciples understood that He was speaking to them about John the Baptist.
When they came to the crowd, a man came up to Jesus and knelt before Him.
“Lord, have mercy on my son,” he said. “He has seizures and is suffering terribly. He often falls into the fire or into the water.
I brought him to Your disciples, but they could not heal him.”
“O unbelieving and perverse generation!” Jesus replied. “How long must I remain with you? How long must I put up with you? Bring the boy here to Me.”
Then Jesus rebuked the demon, and it came out of the boy, and he was healed from that moment.
Afterward the disciples came to Jesus privately and asked, “Why couldn’t we drive it out?”

“Because you have so little faith,” He answered. “For truly I tell you, if you have {{pistis|faith}} the size of a mustard seed, you can say to this mountain, ‘Move from here to there,’ and it will move. Nothing will be impossible for you.”
When they gathered together in Galilee, Jesus told them, “The Son of Man is about to be delivered into the hands of men.
They will kill Him, and on the third day He will be raised to life.” And the disciples were deeply grieved.

units.label: The temple tax
units.note: the free Son who stoops to pay

verses.text: After they had arrived in Capernaum, the collectors of the two-drachma tax came to Peter and asked, “Does your Teacher pay the two drachmas?”

“Yes,” he answered. When Peter entered the house, Jesus preempted him. “What do you think, Simon?” He asked. “From whom do the kings of the earth collect customs and taxes: from their own sons, or from others?”
“From others,” Peter answered. “Then the sons are exempt,” Jesus said to him.

“But so that we may not offend them, go to the sea, cast a hook, and take the first fish you catch. When you open its mouth, you will find a four-drachma coin. Take it and give it to them for My tax and yours.”

metamorphoo.gloss: To be transformed, changed in form; the source of the word metamorphosis. On the mountain Jesus' hidden glory shines through His humanity, a momentary unveiling of who He truly is. The same verb describes the believer's inner transformation, Romans 12:2, 2 Corinthians 3:18.

agapetos.gloss: Beloved, dearly loved. The Father's word from the cloud, My beloved Son, echoes the baptism and marks Jesus as the unique, cherished Son to whom all must listen.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust in God. Even faith as small as a mustard seed, if it is real reliance on God's power, accomplishes what is humanly impossible; the disciples failed not for lack of technique but for lack of faith.

basic.context: Jesus takes Peter, James, and John up a high mountain and is transfigured, His face shining like the sun. Moses and Elijah appear, and the Father's voice declares, This is My beloved Son; listen to Him. Coming down, Jesus explains that Elijah has already come in John the Baptist. He heals a boy the disciples could not, teaching that even mustard-seed faith moves mountains. He foretells His death a second time, and He provides the temple tax from a fish's mouth.
basic.hermeneutics: The transfiguration confirms Peter's confession and frames the whole road to the cross: the suffering Son is also the radiant Lord. Moses and Elijah, the Law and the Prophets, point to Him and then withdraw so only Jesus remains. The valley scenes show what discipleship needs, faith, and who Jesus is, the free Son of the King who still stoops.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus is metamorphoo, transfigured, His glory shining out. The Father names Him agapetos, beloved. And the disciples fail for want of pistis, faith, which even mustard-seed small can move mountains.
basic.typology: The shining face recalls Moses on Sinai; the mountain, the cloud, and the voice recall God's presence at Sinai. Jesus is the prophet like Moses to whom Israel must listen. Moses and Elijah, who both met God on a mountain, now meet the One they foretold.
basic.christ: Jesus is the beloved Son, radiant with divine glory, greater than Moses and Elijah, the one to whom the Law and the Prophets give way. He is the suffering Son of Man and the exempt Son of the King who still pays the tax, humble and free at once.
basic.god: The Father speaks from the cloud, confirming and commanding: this is My Son, listen to Him. God unveils glory to strengthen faith for the coming suffering, and He meets terror with the tender word through Jesus, do not be afraid.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 24 and 34 give the mountain, cloud, and shining face. Deuteronomy 18 promises a prophet like Moses to whom Israel must listen. Malachi 4 promises Elijah before the day of the Lord. 2 Peter 1 recalls the majesty on the holy mountain.
basic.oneStory: At the hinge of the Gospel, the Father unveils the glory of the beloved Son and commands the world to listen to Him. The suffering foretold in the previous chapter is set against the shining truth of who Jesus is. Then He descends into a faithless world to heal, to foretell His death again, and to pay a tax He does not owe, the glorious Son walking the humble road to the cross.

context.literary: The transfiguration comes six days after Peter's confession and the first passion prediction, binding glory and suffering together. It fulfills Jesus' word that some would see the Son of Man in His kingdom. Matthew then descends through a cluster of scenes, the failed exorcism, the second passion prediction, and the temple tax, that test and teach the disciples about faith, suffering, and Jesus' identity.
context.historical: The high mountain is traditionally Tabor or, more likely given the setting, a spur of Mount Hermon near Caesarea Philippi. Booths recall the Feast of Tabernacles. The two-drachma tax was the annual half-shekel every Jewish man paid for the upkeep of the temple, rooted in Exodus 30; the stater found in the fish was a four-drachma coin, exactly enough for two.
context.canonical: The scene is saturated with Sinai imagery, Exodus 24 and 34, and presents Jesus as the prophet like Moses of Deuteronomy 18:15, to whom Israel must listen. Elijah's coming fulfills Malachi 4:5-6 in John the Baptist. Peter later appeals to this eyewitness glory, 2 Peter 1:16-18, as confirmation of the prophetic word.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal the divine glory of the beloved Son and confirm Peter's confession, binding Jesus' coming suffering to His true majesty, and to teach the disciples about faith and the humble freedom of the Son.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The transfiguration describes a unique revelation, not a pattern to seek. The Father's command, listen to Him, is directly prescriptive, as is the lesson that real faith, however small, relies on God's power. The temple tax models a wise, non-offensive submission that is not compromise.

debates.question: In what sense had Elijah already come?

views.label: Fulfilled in John the Baptist
views.case: Jesus says Elijah has already come and they did not recognize him, and the disciples understood He meant John, who came in the spirit and power of Elijah to prepare the way, fulfilling Malachi 4.

views.label: A further restoration still awaited
views.case: Jesus also says Elijah does indeed come and will restore all things, which some take to leave room for a fuller end-time fulfillment beyond John, alongside the fulfillment already given.

debates.bearing: The plain sense is that John fulfills the Elijah promise; whether one also expects a further restoration, the point stands that the forerunner was rejected, just as the Son of Man will suffer.

study.originalLanguages: On the mountain Jesus is metamorphoo, transfigured, His hidden glory breaking through His flesh so that His face shines like the sun. The Father's voice names Him agapetos, the beloved Son, and commands, listen to Him, so that the Law and the Prophets, seen in Moses and Elijah, give way to the Son alone. In the valley the disciples fail to cast out a demon, and Jesus traces the failure to their small pistis, their faith; yet faith no bigger than a mustard seed, if it truly leans on God's power, moves mountains. Glory above and faith below meet in the one beloved Son who is going to the cross.

typology.fulfillment: Jesus' face shining with an inherent glory, greater than the reflected glory of Moses, 2 Corinthians 3:7-18.
Jesus, the Son to whom the Father says listen, fulfilling Deuteronomy 18:15.

typology.type: Elijah before the day of the LORD
typology.fulfillment: John the Baptist, the forerunner in the spirit of Elijah, Malachi 4:5-6.

study.christ: Jesus is the beloved Son unveiled in glory, His face like the sun, greater than Moses and Elijah, who appear only to point to Him and then vanish so that He is seen alone. The Father's command, listen to Him, sets the Son above every other voice. Yet the glory does not cancel the cross; it confirms it, for the radiant Lord descends at once to a faithless world, foretells His death a second time, and provides the temple tax He does not owe. He is the free Son of the King who stoops to pay, the majestic Son who walks willingly to Jerusalem, glorious and humble in one person.
study.god: The Father Himself speaks from the bright cloud, confirming the Son and commanding that He be heard, so that the disciples' confession is sealed by heaven. God gives a glimpse of glory to steady faith for the suffering ahead, and when the disciples fall in terror it is the Son, sent by the Father, who touches them and says do not be afraid. God is the King whose Son is rightly exempt, yet who chooses not to give needless offense, teaching His people a wise and gracious submission.

crossReferences.note: A prophet like Moses whom you must listen to, echoed in the Father's command.
Moses' face shining after meeting God, the backdrop to Jesus' transfiguration.
Elijah sent before the great day, fulfilled in John the Baptist.
Peter's eyewitness memory of the Majestic Glory and the voice on the holy mountain.

study.oneStory: At the turning point of the Gospel, heaven confirms the confession: the suffering Christ is the beloved, glorious Son, and the world is told to listen to Him. Moses and Elijah, the whole Old Testament, hand the story to Jesus and step back. Then the radiant Son descends to a faithless valley to heal, to foretell His death, and to pay a tax He does not owe, walking the humble road that leads to the cross and, beyond it, to the glory glimpsed on the mountain.

quiz.q: What did the Father's voice from the cloud declare?

options.text: This is My beloved Son; listen to Him, confirming Jesus' glory and authority.
options.note: Verse 5 gives the Father's affirmation and command.

options.text: That Jesus was a great prophet like Moses and Elijah.
options.note: Jesus is the prophet like Moses, but the voice sets Him above them as the beloved Son, not merely alongside them.

options.text: That the disciples should build three shelters on the mountain.
options.note: That was Peter's suggestion, which the voice effectively overrides by pointing only to the Son.

options.text: That Moses and Elijah were equal to Jesus.
options.note: They withdraw so that only Jesus remains; He is the beloved Son to be heard.

quiz.q: Why did Moses and Elijah appear, and then only Jesus remained?

options.text: The Law and the Prophets bear witness to Jesus and then give way to Him alone.
options.note: Their appearing and withdrawing show all Scripture pointing to the Son, seen alone in verse 8.

options.text: To show that Jesus stood in the line of Israel's great leaders.
options.note: He fulfills that line, but the scene shows Him surpassing it, not merely joining it.

options.text: To give the disciples three teachers to follow equally.
options.note: The Father commands them to listen to the Son, not to three equal voices.

options.text: To prove that Moses and Elijah had never really died.
options.note: The point is Jesus' glory and supremacy, not speculation about the two prophets.

quiz.q: Why could the disciples not drive out the demon?

options.text: Because of their little faith; real reliance on God's power was lacking.
options.note: Verse 20 names their little faith as the cause.

options.text: Because the demon was an unusually powerful kind.
options.note: The difficulty was real, but Jesus locates the failure in their faith, not the demon's strength.

options.text: Because they had not been given authority to cast out demons.
options.note: They had been given such authority in chapter 10; the issue was faith, not authorization.

options.text: Because Jesus alone could ever cast out demons.
options.note: Jesus expected them to, and taught that faith like a mustard seed would suffice.

quiz.q: What does the temple tax episode reveal about Jesus?

options.text: As the Son of the King He is exempt, yet He freely stoops to pay so as not to give offense.
options.note: Verses 25-27 show His freedom as the Son and His gracious submission.

options.text: That Jesus always obeyed every tax law without exception.
options.note: He does pay, but the point is His freedom as the Son who chooses to pay, not mere law-keeping.

options.text: That Jesus was subject to the temple like everyone else.
options.note: He declares the sons exempt; He pays by gracious choice, not obligation.

options.text: That Jesus refused to pay the temple tax.
options.note: He pays it, providing the coin from the fish's mouth.

questions: The Father commands, listen to Him. Whose voices are competing for your attention, and what would it mean to truly listen to Jesus this week?
The glory of Christ was given to steady the disciples before suffering. How does a fresh sight of who Jesus is strengthen you for what you are facing?
Jesus traced the disciples' failure to little faith. Where are you relying on your own technique or effort instead of trusting God's power?
The radiant Son came down into a faithless valley to heal. How does it encourage you that His glory does not keep Him from your mess and need?
The free Son chose to pay rather than give needless offense. Where might love lead you to yield a right you could rightfully claim?

translationNotes.note: The source separates three shelters from the list with a dash; it is rendered here with a comma for a clean, dash-free reading: one for You, one for Moses, and one for Elijah.

The BSB numbering goes from verse 20 to verse 22; verse 21, this kind does not come out except by prayer and fasting, is not included in the earliest manuscripts and appears as a footnote drawn from Mark 9:29.
The two-drachma tax is the annual half-shekel temple tax of Exodus 30:13; the coin found in the fish is a stater, a four-drachma piece worth about a shekel, enough for both Jesus and Peter.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 17:1-27. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; heading, line-break, and note markers were skipped. Verse 21 is omitted in the BSB (earliest manuscripts) and is carried in a translation note. The em dash in verse 4 was converted to a comma. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
